data.title: Netflix: Stock Approaches Reality
data.description:
Netflix (NFLX) stock is down despite a decent earnings gain. Even cash flow has grown. But this points to an overpriced stock heading towards reality rather than any serious business issues. In fact, the offer to acquire Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) corrects some of the model flaws that were becoming evident for some time. Management has further noted that they intend to allow the businesses they know nothing about to operate with the current management in place. That should increase the chances of merger success materially. Taken together, the business is fine. It is the stock price that needs adjusting. Mr. Market is coming out of one of those periods of irrational exuberance that has marked this stock price since it went public. Netflix will survive. But it will survive with a lower valuation going forward.

Last Article

The last article noted that the acquisition fills some key holes in the Netflix business model to improve the overall business. Today, that offer was raised slightly while being improved to an all-cash offer. That is a backhanded acknowledgment of the declining stock price and quite possibly a fear of what the declining Netflix stock price will do to the offer if it continues.

To me, the market kept overpricing the business substantially by believing that an independent Netflix alone would deliver substantial cash flows after years of never meeting that expectation. Instead, that expectation was always pushed into the future. Years of revenue and earnings growth kept that expectation alive.

Now, it would seem that the offer to acquire Warner Bros Discovery has given the market a reality check that really was hard for Mr. Market to face any other way. As a result, the stock price is now heading towards a realistic valuation.

Stock Price

The stock is actually down roughly 21% since my last article on the earnings update. That was the October earnings update.

Even now, though, the stock price would still indicate that cash flow and earnings should both grow more than 30% a year to justify that price-earnings ratio. But the latest earnings report continues a growth rate that is expected for a large company like this that is nothing close to that price-earnings ratio.

This likely indicates that the stock price has more downside potential. These types of "story" stocks nearly always adjust to reality in the long-term. The major question is if this correction is "the time" or if it is just a period of unusual volatility that will end for the stock to head skyward again.

Remember that any growth story has an ending. With the market cap shown above, it is time for really any investor to ask how long growth can continue. After growth ends, then what is the final valuation of the company? Realistically, the largest company by valuation in the stock market is not all that much larger (compared to when the company went public) in market value. That question needs to be combined with a realistic market share that the company can obtain.

Earnings

The company did post an increase in cash flow, earnings, and revenue. But as usual, the free cash flow was a very small percentage of the market value shown above. A quick comparison to more mature companies and other industries can lead to a suspicion that the cash flow percentage of the market value needs to grow substantially in the near future.

As shown above, growth will continue to be in the teens for revenue compared to the prior year, same quarter. But that implies a price-earnings ratio that should be in the teens. That is less than half of the current stock price. It is nearly impossible for earnings and cash flow to grow faster than revenue in the long-term.

Now that free cash flow is being generated, the all-cash offer of Warner Bros Discovery is a very reasonable proposition going forward. This company is acquiring the growth parts of the company, which are really just beginning to realize their cash flow potential.

That made it very hard for Mr. Market to value future cash flow prospects. But professionals in the industry that manage Netflix have a much easier time identifying the future prospects of the Warner Bros Discovery turnaround project.

There is very likely to be the free cash flow shown above, plus a substantial contribution from the acquired Warner Bros Discovery business to make the total debt repayment a solid proposition.

In the meantime, both businesses will benefit from all the additional ways that Warner Bros. Discovery monetizes content. There could be some very substantial growth there, far in excess of the growth that Netflix alone could generate. But it will take some time to get there. It will not happen overnight.

In the meantime, the growth in cash flow in the Netflix business needs to continue. That is the original premise of the company when it originally went public (that the business would generate a whole lot of cash for investors). Most of the streaming business is really just breaking into free cash flow generation (just like this business). Therefore, there is likely to be a whole lot more cash flow (annually) ahead as this business transitions to a more generic business.

Summary

The earnings announcement shows that the business of Netflix itself continues to grow. But that growth will likely be in the teens for the long-term. That growth rate is far slower than the price-earnings ratio would seem to indicate. Therefore, it is quite possible that the declining stock price indicates a transition to reality.

I very much like the acquisition of the Warner Bros. Discovery business because I believe it improves the Netflix model considerably when it comes to monetizing content. I similarly believe that the all-cash offer is an acknowledgment by management of the possibility that the stock price will continue to decline until that price-earnings ratio approximates the long-term growth rate. But that implies about 50% more downside potential.

There is also the possibility that the market will not like the financially leveraged Netflix that results and therefore assign a discount valuation until the debt is at least somewhat repaid.

For that reason, Netflix is a sell until all of this works out due to my belief that the stock price is still in the overpriced range.

Risks

There is an old Wall Street saying that the market can stay irrational longer than you can stay solvent. Should that prove to be the case here, the Netflix stock price could again turn around to head towards new highs. It would not be the first time something like that would happen.

Any acquisition, including the Warner Bros. Discovery acquisition, can fail to meet market expectations. Now in the beginning, the combined results will reflect the improving conditions of the acquired business. However, in the long-term, there will be some combination of the respective businesses to allow more monetization channels of the content of both companies.

This management has stated many times that it will allow those businesses of Warner Bros Discovery to operate independently because Netflix is not in those businesses. That is a very smart move that would increase the chances of merger success sharply. There is always the risk, though, that management backtracks on that idea and starts to meddle in businesses it knows nothing about. That could cause very serious future issues, including a "brain-drain".

data.title: PICK: Offering Global Exposure To Industrial Metals
data.description:
Fast Facts

iShares MSCI Global Metals & Mining Producers ETF (PICK) was launched on 1/31/2012 and tracks the MSCI ACWI Select Metals & Mining Producers Ex Gold & Silver IMI. PICK has a portfolio of 235 stocks, a 30-day SEC yield of 1.85%, a trailing 12-month distribution rate of 2.28%, and an expense ratio of 0.35%. Distributions are paid semi-annually. It is a large and liquid ETF, with $1.6 billion of AUM (assets under management) and an average daily dollar volume of $31 million. The fund’s issuer, iShares, is a part of BlackRock, Inc. (BLK).

Underlying Index

As described by iShares in the prospectus, the underlying index includes…

… equity securities of companies in both developed and emerging markets that are primarily involved in the extraction or production of diversified metals, the production of aluminum or steel, and in the mining of precious metals and minerals (excluding gold and silver).

Companies of all sizes in the MSCI ACWI Investable Market Index are eligible. The index is weighted by free float–adjusted market capitalization and reconstituted semi-annually, with occasional inclusions and exclusions triggered by corporate events or GICS reclassification. This article will be used as a benchmark for the U.S.-focused fund SPDR® S&P Metals and Mining ETF (XME). XME includes gold and silver miners.

Portfolio

PICK has almost half of its asset value in diversified metals and mining companies (48%) and significant exposure in the steel sub-industry (27%). Other sub-industries are below 15%.

Australia is the heaviest country in the portfolio (20.6%), followed by the U.S. (16%) and the U.K. (15.5%). Other countries are below 8%.

The portfolio is quite concentrated. Indeed, the top holdings, listed in the next table, represent 43.6% of asset value, and the heaviest position weighs 11.1%.

Performance

PICK has underperformed XME by 2.7% annualized between 2/7/2012 and 1/16/2026, with a lower risk based on maximum drawdown and standard deviation of monthly return (“volatility” in the table below).

Competitors

The next table compares characteristics of PICK, XME, and four metals and mining ETFs, excluding gold and silver:

COPX is the largest of these funds in assets under management, and XME is the most liquid in dollar volume. METL is the least concentrated in the top holdings and is actively managed, but its track record is too short to assess it.

Takeaway: who should consider PICK?

PICK offers indirect exposure to industrial metals without the bias of commodity futures (contango/backwardation). Excluding gold and silver mining companies has pros and cons. It removes the noise due to precious metal speculation and lowers volatility, but it also harms long-term returns. Anyway, PICK is uncompelling as a long-term investment, based on its historical performance and risk metrics. This fund is best-suited for swing trading and tactical allocation.

This article answers these three main questions about PICK:

  • How is PICK’s portfolio structured?
  • How does PICK compare to an industry benchmark?
  • What type of strategies is PICK best suited for?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: World Markets Watchlist: January 20, 2026
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Our global markets watchlist tracks nine prominent indexes from economies around the world. The list includes the S&P 500 from the United States, TSX from Canada, the FTSE 100 from England, the DAXK from Germany, the CAC 40 from France, the Nikkei 225 from Japan, the Shanghai from China, the Hang Seng from Hong Kong, and the BSE SENSEX from India.

Six of the nine indexes on our world markets watch list posted year-to-date gains through January 20, 2026. Japan's Nikkei 225 is in the top spot with a year-to-date gain of 5.3%. China's Shanghai is in second with a year-to-date gain of 3.7% while Hong Kong's Hang Seng is in third with a year-to-date gain of 3.3%. On the opposite end, India's BSE SENSEX is the index with the largest year-to-date loss, currently at -3.6%.

To provide additional context on where these indexes stand relative to their historical peaks, the table below shows each index’s current value, all-time peak, the date of that peak, and how far it is from that record level.

World Indexes and Recent Recessions

Let's start with a very recent chart with the latest recession. We've used February 3, 2020, for our start date (this is the official NBER recession start).

The chart below illustrates the comparative performance of world markets since March 9, 2009. The start date is arbitrary: The S&P 500, TSX, CAC 40 and BSE SENSEX hit their lows on March 9th, the Nikkei 225 on March 10th, the DAXK on March 6th, the FTSE on March 3rd, the Shanghai Composite on November 4, 2008, and the Hang Seng even earlier on October 27, 2008.

However, by aligning on the same day and using a log-scale vertical axis, we get an excellent visualization of the relative performance. I've indexed each of the eight to 800 on the March 9th start date. The callout in the upper left corner shows the percent change from the start date to the latest weekly close.

Here is the same visualization, this time starting on October 9, 2007, a previous closing high for the S&P 500. This date is also approximately the mid-point of the range of market peaks, which started on June 1st for the CAC 40 and ended on January 8, 2008 for the SENSEX.

For a longer look at the relative performance, our final chart starts at the turn of the century, again indexing each at 800 for the start date.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Stocks Rebound: Dow Jones And U.S. Stock Index Outlook
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Wise traders usually refrain from trying to make sense of market movement – and rightfully so. After two consecutive rejection days for US indexes (and their futures as stock markets were closed yesterday), dip buyers are coming right back to the market.

Opening 2% below their Friday close on average, stock indexes were struggling to find any optimism in the headlines before US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent saved the day.

His latest comments eased the pessimistic sentiment, saying that he is "urging everyone here to [...] take a deep breath, and let things play out", and not to retaliate against the US.

A passive-aggressive tone from the Treasury secretary, but traders seem to be leaning more to the passive side. My own thesis is that it could be a gigantic farce to distract the world from Iran, but it's just a theory.

  • In case you missed it, Bessent also pinpointed next week as the day the world will learn the President's choice for the next Fed Chair. This will be a key event for upcoming sessions as traders prepare for earnings season.

In any case, US stock futures were down ~1.50% at one point in the morning session and are now coming right back to unchanged – a significant shift in sentiment.

US spot indexes are still down around 1%, catching up with yesterday's red day in futures trading.

Intel (INTC) is one of the only green stocks in today's session – the large government stake in the company allows it to rebound well. However, looking at the rest of the market, the picture is bleak.

Mag 7s are struggling quite harshly, but things could get better as we near their earnings season – it will, of course, depend on whether they can beat their high expectations.

Netflix (NFLX) is reporting today after the close, Intel reports on Thursday, and for the majority of other mega caps, participants will have to wait until next Wednesday.

We will explore as we dive into our daily session charts and trading levels for the major US indexes: Dow Jones, Nasdaq, and S&P 500.

Dow Jones 4H Chart

The 4H 200-period moving average is coming at clutch time to save the day, particularly as Scott Bessent timed his comments well, leading to a 0.80% bounce.

  • Still, bulls will have to push today's session close above the key momentum pivot at 48,870 to push sentiment higher and confirm the TACO (headlines would tend to follow the rebound).
  • Failing to break above the pivot zone points to further downside, where the 48,000 major support level could be attained swiftly.
    • Below this, the Dow enters bearish territory.

Except for Thursday's PCE data and GDP data, there won't be much other than a de-escalation between the EU and US to pump markets.

Dow Jones technical levels for trading:

Resistance Levels

  • Christmas ATH high timeframe momentum pivot as resistance: 48,870
  • Session highs: 48,941
  • 49,650 to 49,670 Current ATH Resistance
  • All-time highs: 49,710
  • 50,000 Potential Psychological Resistance

Support Levels

  • Session lows and 4H 200-MA: 48,530
  • November ATH 48,300 to 48,500 support
  • Psychological support at 48,000
  • 45,000 psychological level (Main Support on higher timeframe)

Nasdaq 4H Chart

As indicated in our Friday index analysis, Nasdaq has remained in a large range between 24,500 and 25,800 since the end of November. Now reacting strongly to its 25,000 mini support zone, bulls will want to confirm the range by retesting at least its newly formed downward channel highs (around 25,500).

With the 4H 50 and 200-period MAs acting as resistances there, a push beyond would relaunch the Nasdaq to some new highs. Failing to do so could see further profit-taking/ rangebound price action for the index.

Nasdaq technical levels of interest:

Resistance Levels

  • Momentum Pivot: 25,200 to 25,500 +/- 75 pts
  • 4H MA 50 and 200 at ~25,500
  • Intermediate Resistance: 25,700 to 25,850
  • All-time high resistance zone: 26,100 to 26,300
  • Current ATH: 26,182

Support Levels

  • Minor support: 25,000 to 25,250
  • Session lows: 24,977
  • 24,500 Main support
  • Early 2025 ATH at 22,000 to 22,229 support

S&P 500 4H Chart

The Spoose was showing strong resilience, looking the most favorable technically to its peers. However, things have changed as the triangle formation presented on Friday has been broken to the downside, and today's rebound is seeing harsh rejection at its 6,880 momentum pivot – the outlook for the S&P 500 is now bearish.

The 4H MA 200 (6,887) will act as major resistance.

  • Breaking back above brings back bullish hopes.
  • A rejection there, however, could quickly lead to a test of the mid-December lows at 6,729.
    • Watch the latest headlines to see if they corroborate with more uncertainty or an actual TACO (which would be bullish).

S&P 500 technical levels of interest:

Resistance Levels

  • Pivot zone: 6,880 to 6,900
  • 4H 200-period MA at 6,887
  • Previous ATH resistance: 6,945 to 6,975
  • Current ATH resistance at 7,000

Support Levels

  • Session lows: 6,820
  • 6,800 Psychological Support
  • Support 6,720 to 6,750 at mid-December lows at 6,729
  • 6,400 major psychological support

Safe Trades!

Original Post

data.title: Fidelis Insurance: Patience Will Be Rewarded Given Valuation
data.description:
Shares of Fidelis Insurance Holdings Limited (FIHL) have been a moderate performer over the past year, gaining about 10%. More recently, though, shares have pulled back about 5% from their highs, and they were downgraded to “underweight” by analysts at JPMorgan. Since I last covered Fidelis in October, shares have traded roughly flat, lagging the market’s 3% gain, which is disappointing considering my “Strong Buy” rating. My view was that 2025 was a transition year as it closed the book on Russian aviation claims, providing room for shares to rally in 2026. Now is a good time to see if this thesis is still intact, given some pessimism surrounding shares. I believe it is.

Fidelis is a diversified insurance company that primarily focuses on the insurance market, with a still-sizeable reinsurance business. It focuses on niche markets like marine, aviation, and select property markets. FIHL has an exclusive 10-year rolling partnership with the Fidelis partnership. Under this arrangement, FIHL has access to policies underwritten by a handful of partners. FIHL has the right of first refusal to provide capital and take on these policies, and any policies that it does not view favorably, it can pass on, and its underwriting partners seek other capital providers for them. This arrangement provides FIHL with access to the most attractive policies, and while it essentially outsources the initial underwriting process, the long-term and rolling nature of the agreement provides a steady pipeline of policies. FIHL is also actively working to expand the number of partners inside the program to further ensure its longevity and attractiveness.

This disappointing performance in shares has come even as the company reported excellent results in its third quarter, earning $1.21 per share and beating estimates by $0.20. During the quarter, FIHL generated an excellent 21.4% return on equity, given both solid underwriting results as well as favorable investment outcomes. In total, it reported a 79% combined ratio. This was dramatically better than the 87.4% reported a year earlier. This was an unsustainably strong result. Catastrophe and large losses were just $57 million, as no hurricanes made landfall on the continental United States. Over time, there will be hurricanes that hit the United States' most years, resulting in higher losses. This quiet hurricane season was more akin to a one-time benefit in my view.

Insurance premiums grew by 4%, led by strength in direct property policies to $606 million, and its combined ratio improved 7% to 68.9%. As a result, underwriting income jumped $28 million to $142 million—over two-thirds of this improvement was tied to the catastrophe environment. Reinsurance premiums jumped $32 million to $192 million, and its combined ratio plunged 14% to 32%, entirely due to the catastrophe environment with results stable otherwise. Premium growth was thanks to stronger pricing and opportunities after the significant losses in early 2025 from the California wildfires. Given fears around moderating insurance margins, the fact FIHL is seeing flat-to-improving results ex-catastrophes speaks to the resilience and attractiveness of its underwriting program.

Net investment income was $46 million, down from $52 million last year, primarily due to lower assets, given its payout of now-settled Russian aviation claims and share repurchases. There had been a multiyear battle between insurers and aircraft owners over the planes stranded in Russia following the Ukraine war. This event was a true “black swan” event in my view, and I do not view losses associated with it as being a result of poor underwriting. Importantly, FIHL’s claims are now all settled. In fact, in Q3, we saw $16 million of favorable prior year developments now that it no longer faced unfavorable Russia developments.

Importantly, the company maintains a solid investment portfolio with limited risk. In its $4.4 billion portfolio, it keeps 25% in cash to ensure liquidity for claims, though with the Fed cutting rates, this will pressure interest earned on this allocation. Still, its total portfolio has a 2.7-year duration, and as fixed income securities mature, reinvestment yields are likely to be slightly higher, offsetting some of the cash yield pressure. With an A+ average rating, the portfolio also has very limited credit risk.

FIHL also has a solid balance sheet, with a 26% debt-to-capital ratio; I want to see this ratio remain below 30%. This enables FIHL to return capital to investors. Shares offer a 3.3% dividend yield. Book value ended Q3 at $23.29, well above where shares are trading, which has made buybacks attractive. During Q3, it bought back $32 million of stock and continued to be actively repurchasing shares in Q4. FIHL has $153 million available on its buyback program, and I expect this to be fully exhausted by mid-to-late 2026.

Now, one fair point of caution is that 40% of shares are held from its pre-IPO investor base. Should these investors decide to begin exiting positions, it could create meaningful technical pressure in shares as the publicly available float increases dramatically. With shares trading well below book value, I do not view significant sales as likely, though this prospect could keep a lid on shares. I view this as the biggest potential risk to shares.

I am not the only one who finds FIHL’s valuation compelling. Shares get a favorable “A-“ rating from Seeking Alpha’s valuation tool, in large part due to its low P/E and price-to-book multiples. Now, valuations can stay cheap for prolonged periods, but this does provide patient investors with a margin of safety. Additionally, with FIHL generating capital, it can buy back stock at a discounted valuation to take advantage of the situation, further increasing book value per share and potential upside. While FIHL has overhangs from potential insider selling and a complex corporate structure, these are more than fully reflected in valuation, in my view.

Back in October, I was targeting $22.50-$23, or about 1x book value, given my belief that reserve builds earlier in 2025 were really a one-time occurrence. In fact, book value has grown even more dramatically, aided by accretive repurchases and the lack of hurricanes. Given its return profile, shares could justify a 1.2-1.4x book multiple, but given its smaller size and ownership, I still view 1x book as a fair price target, and I do believe shares should pass $23. The longer shares remain lower, the more I expect FIHL to focus on buybacks, which only further drives up book value.

As such, I'm keeping a "Strong Buy" rating on FIHL and view the recent pullback as unmerited. With each quarter of solid results, the Russia losses will fade from memories, and more focus will be paid on ongoing results. This should gradually narrow the valuation discount, aided by buybacks providing technical support. FIHL may require some patience, but I believe that patience will be rewarded with over 25% upside to fair value alongside a secure dividend.

data.title: Packaging Corporation of America: Positives Are Priced In (Downgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Packaging Corporation of America (PKG) have been a poor performer over the past year, losing about 6% of their value. The entire packaging sector has faced pressure from weak demand and excess supply, leading to some production shut-ins. Given its strong balance sheet and accretive M&A, PKG has held in better than some peers, but the macro environment has clearly been a headwind. I last covered shares of Packaging Corp in September, when I upgraded shares to a “Hold,” and since then, the stock has gained 5%, matching the market’s performance and justifying the rating. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit shares. I suggest exiting PKG.

Packaging Corp is one of the leading makers of corrugated packaging in the United States. Following its acquisition of Greif, it now has mills and plants across much of the US. Greif’s complementary asset base should provide meaningful opportunity for cost cuts and synergies, which should pass $75 million and be fully realized on a run-rate basis by the end of this year.

Q4 Is Tracking As Planned

In a December update, PKG reported that the quarter is going in line with expectations, though acquired Greif assets are performing better than initially hoped, which is an encouraging sign as this is the company’s most meaningful purchase in over a decade. While management will provide more specific guidance in its Q4 release, PKG expects 2026 cap-ex to be similar to 2025 levels, or about $800 million, with some of this spending focused on updating Greif’s assets to PKG’s industry-leading specifications.

PKG is set to report earnings after the market closes on Jan. 27. Analysts expect the company to earn $2.41 per share, though expectations have been coming down with 7 estimate cuts over the past quarter. Results have been mixed lately, reflecting a challenging operating environment. PKG has missed two of its last four earnings releases. Before that, it only missed twice over sixteen quarters. Given my expectation for strong realization of Greif synergies, I expect PKG to earn about $2.45.

Operating Performance Is Solid

Recapping recent results, in the company’s third quarter, Packaging Corp earned $2.73 per share, which missed estimates by $0.09. Q3 results were impacted by one month of Greif operations, which detracted $0.11, as there were initially extended outages upon closing of the deal to do some maintenance work and bring facilities to PKG’s standards. The first quarter an acquisition occurs often creates noise in results, which is what happened here. Absent Greif, results were a bit better than expected, which I view as being a more accurate depiction of how PKG’s underlying business performed.

EBITDA was $503 million, up from $61 million last year. Its packaging segment reported a 23.1% margin, up 90bps from last year. This reflects cost-cutting efforts, as well as stabilization in demand, which more than offset pressure from electricity prices. The company saw cautious ordering patterns, though demand improved during the quarter. The company aims to bring inventory down over the next 6–9 months, which should support free cash flow. Q3 free cash flow was $277 million, and PKG has generated $601 million year to date.

The margin performance was very strong because PKG has seen electricity rates rise 50%-75% over the past two years. Given the energy intensity of the packaging business, this has been a concern I have previously flagged as potentially weighing on margins. The company is actively working on plans to source electricity in-house and take facilities off the grid to reduce the exposure to rising rates. I expect this to be an ongoing trend in the manufacturing sector. While electricity costs are a clear margin risk, I expect 2026 to be the peak of this pressure, given ongoing mitigation efforts. There is a risk this could persist for a bit longer, however.

Q4 orders were tracking up 1% with most segments growing while its beef and building materials verticals face pressure. Beef demand has been weak given substantially higher prices. However, with the Administration easing trade policy with Brazil, I believe we are at or near a bottom here. I am more cautious on construction-related activity, as I do not expect a bottom until late 2026. That said, with mortgage rates now approaching 6%, the pace of decline should be moderating, and I feel stronger conviction a bottom will occur this calendar year. Overall, this points to modest organic sales growth in 2026 on a consolidated basis.

Importantly, in a cyclical business, the company has an excellent financial position. PKG ended Q3 with $806 million of cash on its balance sheet. PKG has $4 billion of debt from $2.5 billion last year. Still, net leverage is only about 1.6x, which is fairly low. PKG used its extremely under-levered balance sheet to add Greif assets at an attractive valuation while still leaving its balance sheet in a reasonable position. As such, I expect much of its free cash flow to be focused on capital returns. Shares offer a 2.3% dividend yield, and we could see up to $200 million of buybacks in 2026.

Valuation More Than Reflects These Positives

Similar to when I last covered PKG, shares do not score particularly well on Seeking Alpha’s valuation screener. Across nearly every metric, shares trade at a premium to both the sector and PKG’s own five-year history. Now, I believe PKG merits a premium to the rest of the sector, given its solid balance sheet, strong operating history, and track record of solid execution. That said, a premium to its own history is harder to justify. Some of the premium reflects the fact that Greif benefits will take time to fully ramp, but shares appear to be fully reflecting them already. That said, I view valuation as full and capping potential upside.

Back in August, I was targeting $10-$10.50 in 2025 EPS. The Greif acquisition should add about $1.25 of earnings power. I now expect the company to earn $10 this year. With 0%-2% organic growth and ramping benefits from Grief, I am targeting $11.25 in 2025 EPS, +/- $0.20. This leaves shares just under 20x earnings and with a ~4%-4.5% free cash flow yield.

PKG Is Now A "Sell"

This is expensive, given PKG is ultimately a commodity business, and long-term, volume growth is likely to be less than 2%. To me, it seems that shares are giving full credit for the company’s positive attributes, and I fear we are now in a similar position to early last year with a full valuation, leaving shares vulnerable to profit taking. Within the sector, I would rather own a stock like Sonoco (SON), which is still a self-help story and has multiple expansion possibilities as synergies of past dealmaking begin to appear, whereas PKG shares already reflect the benefits of its dealmaking.

With a full valuation and a muted macro backdrop, I am moving PKG back to a “Sell.” I do not see tons of downside—I view ~$200-$210 or ~18x earnings and a ~5% free cash flow yield as fair value. That suggests up to 10% downside. Beyond modest downside, though, I see limited scope for outperformance given a full multiple and PKG already trading at such a premium. This lack of upside potential leaves me seeing little reason to own PKG, even as I view the operational performance of the business as strong. I recommend exiting shares and looking elsewhere, like SON, for better opportunities.

data.title: Netflix's 37% Decline Creates A Compelling Entry Point
data.description:
Introduction

I have not covered the popular, long-term outperforming consumer entertainment company Netflix, Inc. (NFLX) before. The stock has now retreated 37% from its highs, despite no fundamental changes. Most of it came from uncertainty around its signed offer to acquire Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD). This made me want to analyze the stock further. On top of that, Netflix reported its Q4 2024 financials today in aftermarket trading.

After having looked at the stock, I am initiating coverage with a Buy, thanks to their strong growth prospects as the number one entertainment company, coupled with margin expansion potential at a fair valuation.

Financials

Let's jump into the report. Here's a summary from the shareholder letter:

With our strong Q4 results, we met or exceeded all of our full year 2025 financial objectives. We grew revenue 16% to $45B (+17% on a F/X neutral basis) and we increased our operating margin to 29.5% for the year, up from 26.7% in 2024. We also made great progress growing advertising revenue. In 2025, which was only our third year selling advertising, ad revenue grew by more than 2.5x vs. 2024 to over $1.5 billion.

Their revenue and EPS just barely beat analysts' expectations. It is apparent that the company continues to leverage multiple growth levers. Ad revenue is growing quickly and as they are enhancing their ad offerings using AI:

We continue to harness AI to enhance the experience for our members, and we’re expanding these capabilities to support our creative teams and advertisers. In 2025, we began testing new AI tools to help advertisers create custom ads based on Netflix’s intellectual property, and we plan to build on this progress in 2026.

... which will help to keep this growth at a steady pace. Subscribers continue to grow, as they have just crossed 325M paid memberships, and so does pricing. Netflix is increasingly adding additional verticals to maintain this growth, including gaming, sports, and live events. In my view, this will help them continue to grow revenues in the double digits for years to come.

The likely reason the stock sold off is their outlook for 2026:

For 2026, based on F/X rates as of 1/1/2026, we forecast revenue of $50.7B-$51.7B. This represents 12%-14% year over year growth (or 11%-13% F/X neutral growth), driven by increases in membership and pricing plus a projected rough doubling of ad revenue in 2026 vs. 2025. We’re targeting a 2026 operating margin of 31.5% (based on 1/1/26 F/X rates), up from 29.5% in 2025, which includes approximately $275M of acquisition-related expenses. Our margin forecast also reflects content amortization growth of ~10% in 2026, with higher growth in the first half than the second half due to the timing of title launches. As a result, we expect higher operating income growth in the second half of 2026 than in the first half. We still see plenty of room to increase our margins and our intent is to grow our operating margin each year, although the magnitude of margin expansion will vary year-to-year as we balance reinvesting in our business with improving profitability.

In my view, the outlook seems fine and margin expansion is on the table. This is another significant part of the Netflix thesis. As visible below, for the full year 2025, revenues have grown 15%, while operating income grew 28%, as expenses barely grew. This shows true operating leverage and that content spend can grow much slower than revenue thanks to AI, which can aid in all areas of content generation and distribution.

This is despite Netflix investing in future growth, such as streaming licenses/deals. Like many other quality stocks, the exact timing of when said margin expansion will occur is uncertain, as the company will continue investing when it continues to see future ROI.

We still see plenty of room to increase our margins and our intent is to grow our operating margin each year, although the magnitude of margin expansion will vary year-to-year as we balance reinvesting in our business with improving profitability.

This is the best type of company to be invested in long-term, as complacent ones that prioritize profitability today over future competitiveness are much more likely to be disrupted and underperform.

Cash flows look great too, as free cash flow grew 36% despite higher CapEx. I expect some normalization of these figures moving forward, but this further shows why Netflix is able to finance the planned acquisition, although share buybacks were paused to conserve cash for said purchase, which at current prices, is unfortunate, but a necessary price to pay, as WBD will likely be accretive to Netflix's ecosystem.

Valuation

After purposefully removing values of 100+, the historical average for NFLX's EV/EBIT, which I believe is the best multiple to look at, measuring the core operating performance of the business against its financial strength, it becomes clear that we are currently undervalued by almost 35%, from a pure mean-reversion angle. This includes extreme values such as in late 2020 and mid-2022. Having traded down to such a level largely reduces the risk of exposure in NFLX stock, because more risk is priced in, granting a bigger margin of safety.

Its forward P/E now stands at just 27, which seems more than fair for a company of this quality.

If we take NFLX's 2030 goals of reaching $1T in market cap, the stock's CAGR from today would be 25.5%, which is very attractive.

Risks

Despite the selloff, Netflix still trades at a relatively high valuation, as noted above. While cheap against its own history, a 28 EV/EBIT ratio is elevated against the broader stock market, where even such a value for the P/E ratio, which, after deducting some non-operating expenses, naturally is higher, would be considered very high. Therefore, a lot of what I talked about is still baked into today's price. Execution could therefore not match current expectations and they might miss on their 2030 goals, leading to trust erosion and multiple compression, similar to 2022, when the stock lost over 75% of its value.

Conclusion

Netflix can be a tough stock to own and is one where one needs to trust management's vision and long-term execution, as shown by the 2022 drop due to subscriber growth slowing down. Management knew the correct answer to this however, by cutting down on password sharing and introducing ads, which together have helped deliver fantastic financial results and propelled the stock far beyond 2021 highs.

If bought today and held with patience, I believe it is highly likely we have an outperformer on our hands, as Netflix will leverage its growth levers. Since technical momentum is still fairly negative, however, I would recommend starting a position today but not going all in yet and continuing to accumulate on further dips.

data.title: Summit Hotel Properties: Preferred Stock Valuation In The Context Of The Credit Profile
data.description:
Today's article is about Summit Hotel Properties, Inc. (INN). We will assess INN's credit quality and review its preferred stock using Moody's approach, as well as the company's key financial metrics and capital structure. (For reference, our last article was here.)

INN Basic Information

  • Total Assets (Q3 2025): close to $2.85 billion
  • Current market capitalization: around $564.83 million
  • Total Debt: ~ $1.45 billion
  • Preferred equity: approximately $260 million
  • EBITDA: $216.2 million
  • Debt Maturities:

  • Capital Structure:

  • Portfolio:

Close to 95 hotels/assets, 40 markets, with a total of approximately 14,347 rooms.

Financial Metrics

  • Asset Yield ((total revenue - operating expenses + depreciation and amortization) / total assets): 22%.
  • Asset Coverage Ratio (total assets/debt): 197%.
  • Operating expenses/total revenue (excluding depreciation and amortization): 14%.
  • Interest expenses/debt: 5.6%.

We also calculate the market-adjusted asset yield for greater accuracy. To do so, we use the price-to-book (P/B) ratio, which at the time of writing is 0.59. We will subtract total equity from total assets and add the new one, multiplied by P/B. Based on these adjustments, we derive the market-adjusted asset yield and coverage ratio.

  • Market Adjusted Asset Yield: 27%.
  • Market Adjusted Asset Coverage ratio: 160%.
  • Expected AFFO Yield: 11%

Below are shown the basic valuation metrics:

Preferred Stocks:

INN has two preferred stocks: (INN.PR.E) and (INN.PR.F). Both stocks trade below par currently.

INN.PR.E was issued on 11/02/2017, with a coupon rate of 6.25%, or a $1.56 annual amount. At its IPO date, the spread to the treasury yield was 3.64%, while currently it is close to 3.33%. Its call date was 11/13/2022, so it is callable anytime.

INN.PR.F's issued date is 08/05/2021. It pays a cumulative distribution of 5.88% per annum, or a $1.47 annual amount. The spread to the treasury yield at the IPO of the stock was 4.11%, and at the time of writing, it is around 3.05%. It has close to half a year to call with a call date on 08/12/2026.

Below are shown charts with the potential yields versus the potential prices of both stocks.

INN.PR.E:

INN.PR.F:

And below you can see where the stocks currently stand among their peers:

Credibility

We will use a Moody's methodology and will try to give a credit rating to INN and its preferred stocks:

  1. Gross assets: Baa3. The gross assets are close to $3.83 billion.
  2. Unencumbered assets: A3. 81% of the assets are unencumbered.
  3. Secured debt: Baa3. The secured debt is close to 19% of the gross assets.
  4. Total debt + preferred stock as a percentage of gross assets: Baa3. The company's total debt and preferred stock are close to 44.6% of its total assets.
  5. Net debt to EBITDA: Ba3. We use the total debt here, and with an EBITDA of $216 million, the ratio is close to 7.9.
  6. Fixed charge coverage: Ba2. EBITDA covers interest and preferred stock dividends at a rate of nearly 2 times.
  7. Market positioning, asset quality, and operating environment: Ca3. It is based on the cyclical nature of hotel revenue. There is also a large operational risk.

8. Liquidity and access to capital: The company's operating expenses, excluding depreciation and amortization, are approximately 14% of revenue. EBITDA covers interest expenses and preferred stock dividends by 2 times. Based on these metrics, the company receives a Baa3 rating.

The final adjusted credit score we receive is equivalent to a Ba3 credit rating. We rate the preferred stocks at B2, two notches below INN's credit rating, which is standard practice for hybrid instruments such as preferred stocks.

Conclusion

INN has a large portfolio, but at the same time, the cyclical nature of the hotel sector limits the company's credit profile. Applying Moody's methodology leads to a final result equivalent to a Ba3 credit rating. The preferred stocks are logically rated two notches lower - B2, with higher risk relative to debt, despite their attractive current yields.

data.title: VNQ Vs. RIET: Why Income Beats Growth For REIT Investors In 2026
data.description:
Broader US REITs have entered 2026 in an unusual position. After one of the most aggressive rate hike cycles in 2022-23, rates have stayed higher for longer, and the outlook for 2026 is still not a post-GFC or COVID-styled normalization to zero. However, some growth REITs have factored in the falling rate regime underway and rebounded from the 2022-23 lows. However, while rates are normalizing, demand remains uneven, and growth remains selective - making further multiple expansion uncertain.

This uncertain 2026 outlook for REITs as a broad category - essentially a tug of war between how much valuation expansion has already happened and how the macro is far from being in its most supportive avatar for REITs - triggers my overallocation recommendation to a lesser-known income-focused REIT ETF. Hoya Capital High Dividend Yield ETF (RIET) looks better positioned in the current regime over the longer-term core REIT allocation - Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF Shares (VNQ).

I recommend overweighting RIET in your REIT portfolio while avoiding fresh capital in VNQ as we enter a carry phase in REITs. The rerating phase we have had over the past couple of years may still have legs, but both the macro environment and the rerating progress made so far imply potentially better times for income-focused REIT investors in the near term. In the event of macro easing and indicating value in growth investments, we can start accumulating VNQ again.

Methodology and Portfolio Repercussions

VNQ's methodology is market cap-based. This implies investments in VNQ flow toward the largest and fastest-growing names. Themes such as data centers, towers, and logistics get emphasized over time, positioning VNQ as a reactive (because market caps are backward-looking and join trends after they are well on the way), but a growth vehicle with income as a secondary outcome. This works for long-term investors willing to hold through full cycles and preferably timing a bulk of their entries during stress valuations (difficult sentimentally). VNQ depends on sustained FFO growth from its constituents and sustained access to equity and debt for growth.

RIET aims for income using a rules-based methodology that, first of all, emphasizes yield-based selection - a methodology that does not overlap very well with growth REITs. Hence, long-term holders (unless purely income-focused) may not see RIET as a core allocation. Another aspect of RIET's methodology is that it looks beyond market caps and diversifies across large, mid, and small caps (with more weights allocated for the mid and small cap REITs). Preferreds occupy 10% of the portfolio by design, and dividend champions occupy 15%. Each bucket is equally weighted, making the overall portfolio less concentrated. This absorbs risks due to exposure to smaller cap REITs (buckets where more names are included to reduce weight per holding further compared to the large cap bucket).

Although RIET's top holdings are not more influential than the rest of the portfolio (often affected by drifts between rebalances rather than significant weight differences at the rebalance), I have shown the top holdings as indicative of the kind of portfolio RIET owns. The top 10 holdings in VNQ account for over half the portfolio. RIET, in comparison, holds only ~15% in the top 10 holdings - a more diversified portfolio by its equal-weighted approach for each bucket.

The VNQ portfolio is generally richly valued for its growth positioning. American Tower has a price to forward FFO valuation of around 22x. Prologis is valued at around ~23x and Welltower at ~36x. These valuation levels are far ahead of the reported sector median of ~13.75x. That is not a problem, but it goes to show how the VNQ portfolio is valuation sensitive and requires valuations and FFO growth to keep delivering outperformance. A meaningful portion of that FFO growth and valuation expansion is already visible in their share prices in the current regime of rate normalization. Names like Equinix have earned premium valuations because of the data center narrative driving growth expectations too.

The question here is not about whether the valuations have room to grow or deserve the premiums, but about the embedded growth and valuation expansion expectations that need to work harder given the current macro situation. That is not the case for RIET's holdings. Here, names like National Storage Affiliates, Gaming and Leisure Properties, and Alexandria Real Estate have price to forward FFO ratios of ~15x, ~12.5x, and ~6.5x. Again, it is not about cheaper valuations alone - that valuation gap often persists, and for valid reasons like growth or even portfolio quality. The difference at the broad holding level that is important to note is that the RIET holdings are cash flow-first portfolios. It also includes some mortgage REITs that often trade below book value. As long as the outlook is not that of a crisis where solvency is at stake, such names do not need growth or valuation expansion environments to flourish. In fact, the best way to own this space is through a diversified portfolio, where idiosyncratic risks are well absorbed.

The Performance Yield Trade-off

The total return charts comparing VNQ and RIET show the expected growth outperformance in VNQ. The total return gap is not very high at ~8 percentage points, and there is no systematic trend of the gap expanding. In fact, interestingly, RIET's higher-risk portfolio has been stress tested through the 2022 trough, when it did not show meaningfully larger relative drawdowns.

That performance gap is more than acceptable for income-focused investors, looking at the yield gap between the two ETFs. VNQ has an average yield in the mid-single-digit range, while RIET has consistently delivered around double-digit yields. I have reviewed numerous covered call income ETFs and see the total return underperformance in RIET in lieu of higher yield much more acceptable than I have seen in the case of covered call income plans versus their growth-oriented underlying.

Outlook Also Favors RIET

While the total return versus yield trade-off already favors a small allocation in RIET, especially for income-oriented investors, the outlook for REITs in the near term makes the current setup even more favorable for RIET. With rates staying higher for longer (importantly, uncertain), I find it easier to underwrite stability over valuation expansion.

RIET's portfolio includes mortgage REITs that diversify rate impacts and net lease, gaming, storage, and hospitality bets that are typically cash-rich and have contractual cash flow strength. They are less reliant on capital market enthusiasm. VNQ could do well if cap rates compress meaningfully, equity capital is more accessible, and secular growth narratives like data centers accelerate further. That is not ruled out, but VNQ has to answer those questions in addition to how much is priced in already. RIET does not need to, relatively speaking.

Therefore, I recommend a smaller satellite allocation in RIET to spread risks in the near term while keeping VNQ as the core. Hence, VNQ is a Hold (will upgrade to Buy when macro uncertainties decrease, or further progress is seen in growth narratives). RIET is rated a Buy for the current regime. Income investors can allocate even higher weights to the ETF, even for long-term investments.

The risks to the Buy RIET thesis include any worsening in demand, particularly for experiential and hospitality assets - although they have managed through COVID shutdowns and a demand stress from 2022 onward. Liquidity risks also should be kept in mind - RIET has an AUM of ~$100m. However, for smaller position sizes, liquidity should not impact trades (~79k in average trading volume). The inclusion of mortgage REITs also introduces spread risk and dividend volatility - however, this is not a significant part of the REIT's overall portfolio.

data.title: Where U.S. Growth Went
data.description:
The US economy can no longer generate the real income gains or improvements in the general standard of living it once did, which fueled a sense of optimism and prosperity.

As real income gains continued to fall short of society’s expectations, calls for government solutions grew louder, but ultimately, these efforts worsened the problem.

The main issue is that real income growth is tied to productivity gains, and sustained increases in productivity growth require what’s called “capital deepening,” or more (and better) machinery, equipment, and physical infrastructure for workers, allowing each person to produce more in the same amount of time.

This capital deepening process requires continued investment in physical goods and infrastructure, and over the last several decades, the US economy has invested less and less in these critical areas.

In this post, we’ll explore why the rate of private investment has been declining, how this has impacted our population's standard of living, how we can fix it, and where the economy is heading if we don’t.

How The Standard of Living Improves

The standard of living, or the perception of prosperity, improves when real income grows at a sufficient pace for the average person. Real income, or income after accounting for inflation, is tied to productivity growth.

An economy can only consume what it produces (not counting the trade deficit…we will get there).

If you want to consume more, you have to produce more. Producing more per hour is productivity, and that’s the only way real incomes rise.

If wages go up but productivity doesn’t, prices just rise to match the increase in wages. You earn more dollars, but it buys the same amount of stuff, and nothing changed.

To produce more, it's helpful to have more and better equipment, machinery, or infrastructure to work with.

This chart shows real personal income per capita excluding government transfer payments. Essentially, we are measuring all the income the private sector can generate on its own, adjusted for inflation, per person. At the end of 2025, this amounted to roughly $48,500 per person. We’ll refer to this measure simply as real income.

Real income has increased over time, rising from $20,000 in the early 1970s to almost $49,000 today. It’s essential that real income increases over time, but the rate of increase is what’s really critical and easy to lose sight of.

From the 1960s through 2007, real income increased at a 1.8% pace. After the 2008 recession, real income dropped and never regained the 1.8% trend line as it did after all recessionary periods in the prior four decades.

Had real income growth remained on the 1.8% trendline, the US economy would be generating closer to $53,500 per capita, $5,000 more per capita, or $20,000 more for a family of four.

For many decades, people grew to expect a certain level of real income growth or a certain pace of improvement in their standard of living.

Just as with financial investments in the stock market, small changes in the rate of compounding are hard to see year to year but can grow enormously over many decades.

Shifting the growth rate in real income from 1.8% to 1.3% can’t be felt over one year. But over 18 years, the average person is almost 10% worse off than they would have been had we stayed on the same trend.

What would the average family of four be able to do with an extra $20,000 per year? Reduce debt, pay for child care, increase savings? Or, looked at in reverse, this $20,000 gap is what many families expected in 2005 or 2006 as they projected future income, and this shortfall has been plugged with debt.

It should be clear why most people feel they are not keeping up or their standard of living is not improving as fast as they expected, because for the average person, it’s not. This becomes even worse when looking at the bottom percentage of the income spectrum, since the averages are actually improved by the very wealthy. But why is it happening?

We know that real income is tied to productivity and that real income growth has shifted down. Even a decline from a 1.8% trend to a 1.3% trend has created problems that have grown very large over the last two decades.

This problem has its roots in a lack of investment.

Net private investment measures how much the private sector is investing in structures, equipment, and intellectual property after accounting for depreciation. The economy has to spend a certain amount of money just to stand still because roads, machinery, and buildings get worn out over time, and money needs to be spent simply to offset this erosion.

Net investment measures how much the economy is investing in new structures and equipment after accounting for this wear and tear.

From 1950 to 1980, we invested about 8% of GDP. From 1980 to 2007, we invested about 6% of GDP, and from 2009 to 2025, about 4% of GDP.

If we maintained just a 6% net investment rate, that would be the equivalent of $600 billion of new investment each year - investment that we are not making today.

But why?

There are several reasons for this decline in investment, but they mainly stem from an increase in government size.

Government Size & Net Private Investment

There are certain mathematical identities that describe the economy, and one critical formula is the National Savings & Investment Identity.

The National Savings & Investment Identity explains the relationships among private-sector savings, private-sector investment, government budget deficits, and the trade deficit, with the opposite of the trade deficit being foreign capital inflows.

Domestic saving plus foreign saving must equal private investment plus government borrowing.

This can be written as:

Budget Deficit = Private Savings - Private Investment + Trade Deficit

In other words, if the US budget deficit increases, private savings must increase, private investment must fall, or the trade deficit must widen (or some combination of the three).

Since the US budget deficit has increased faster than the trade deficit, it’s been necessary for private savings to increase or private investment to fall.

Private saving is the opposite of private consumption, and in the United States, we are not keen on reducing consumption, so the burden has led to a decline in private investment.

Beyond the mathematical identity that has caused a decline in net private investment due to larger government budget deficits, there are also shifting incentives arising from a larger government spending firehose.

When government spending and deficits rise as a share of GDP, private investors face increasing uncertainty about future taxes, regulations, and the rules of the game. Long-term capital projects like factories, machinery, and infrastructure require committing resources today for returns that materialize over decades, exposing investors to years of unpredictable policy risk.

Rational actors respond by shortening investment horizons, maximizing liquidity, and favoring financial engineering over real engineering.

Talent flows toward rent-seeking behavior and navigating government rather than building productive capacity.

The result is a secular decline in net investment, a slower-growing capital stock, and compounding productivity erosion.

Fixing the Problem: Is It Even Possible?

Of course, the growing government size and budget deficits are not the only reasons the rate of net investment has declined in the United States, but they are certainly the largest factors.

Fixing the problem is extremely difficult if we agree that the deficit is the largest driver, because the United States is structurally locked into higher spending through Social Security, Medicare, and Medicaid, which make up the bulk of what are called transfer payments.

Transfer payments and interest are now 58% of all government spending, which is more than $11 trillion when factoring in federal, state, and local governments.

If reducing spending is not politically feasible, increasing taxes can close the budget deficit, but that is an equally unpopular political platform.

If we assume government budget deficits will grow in the future, then the trade deficit must increase to maintain our current rates of consumption and domestic investment.

Tariff policy is designed to close the trade deficit, which is why this is a dangerous path if reducing the budget deficit is not a simultaneous plan.

Even still, it’s unlikely the trade deficit can grow at the same speed as the budget deficits are growing, even if we wanted to. So the mathematical result is lower consumption or lower investment, or some combination of the two.

A continued decline in the rate of net investment is almost assured.

Where We Are Going & What You Can Do About It

As the rate of net investment continues to stair-step lower as it has over the last several decades, productivity growth will falter.

A lower and lower rate of net investment over time means worse and less reliable roads, airports, power grids, machinery, train stations, power plants, and factories.

Decaying infrastructure leads to lower productivity outcomes, which in turn lead to lower rates of real income growth.

This chart shows the percentage of time real income growth exceeded 3% over rolling 10-year periods.

So in the early 1970s, real income growth exceeded 3% more than 60% of the time. Today, real income growth has exceeded 3% just 23% of the time in the last 10 years.

This will continue to slide, reducing the average person's sense of prosperity and leaving out the people at the bottom of the income structure.

Savings equals investment, and the core issue is that the US doesn’t save.

Savings is essential, and one thing you can do is zig while everyone is zagging. Increasing your personal savings rate is likely the best antidote to a society with a savings deficiency.

Wealth and power will accumulate to the companies that are capital-light and monopoly-like in pricing power, which is why these well-known companies have been and likely will continue to be strong investments. But importantly, these gains have been built on an increasingly unstable foundation that will continue to erode if we remain on this same path, which many find unavoidable.
